title: Chapter 42: The Screen
content: The house is full of people.

I stand at the edge of the entrance hall and listen to them — the low hum of conversation, the clink of ceramic mugs, a child's laughter spiraling up from somewhere near the therapy wing. Two years ago, this house was a crime scene. Now it smells like fresh coffee and cut flowers, and there are hand-painted signs on the walls that say things like *You Are Safe Here* and *Recovery Is Not a Line*.

The second anniversary of the Bly Recovery Center. Someone thought a memorial service was the right idea. Someone else thought it should be a celebration. They compromised and made it both, which is probably the most honest thing this house has ever done.

I'm not here for business. I'm not here for the past. I'm here because Flora asked me to come, and because I've learned — slowly, painfully, with the kind of grace that doesn't come naturally to me — that showing up matters more than being ready.
A therapist I don't recognize touches my elbow and thanks me for coming. I nod. I smile. I am improving at both. The smile reaches my eyes sometimes now. Not always. But sometimes.

The ceremony is held in what used to be the dining room, now a large group therapy space with folding chairs and soft lighting. A woman named Dr. Anara Sing gives a short speech about the center's work. She mentions the house's history with the careful vagueness of someone who knows the details but has chosen not to make them the point. The Trial of Peter Bly. The whistleblowing that exposed the surveillance network. The survivors who came forward. She doesn't say my name, and I'm grateful.

There are other speakers. A young man who completed the residential program. A mother whose daughter is in outpatient care. A counselor who describes the garden they planted behind the east wing — the same soil, the same earth, now growing vegetables instead of dread.

I sit in the back row. I listen. I let the words wash over me like water that's almost too warm, the kind that makes your skin flush and your muscles unclench. I don't cry. I don't need to. The house is doing that work for both of us — holding what happened, containing it, turning it into something with walls and a purpose instead of an open wound.

After the last speaker, people mill around. Someone puts on music — something acoustic and gentle. The children race through the hallway, and nobody stops them, and the sound of their footsteps is nothing like the sound of footsteps used to be in this house.
I slip out the side door.
The grounds are different.

Not the bones of them — the lake is where it's always been, and the drive still curves up from the main road through the same corridor of oak trees. But someone has planted wildflowers along the front path. The garden where I once found Flora at dawn, sitting in the cold with her knees drawn up and her eyes fixed on nothing, now has a wooden bench and a small greenhouse. A sign on the greenhouse door says *Flora's Garden* in painted letters. She doesn't know I've seen it. Or maybe she does. Maybe that's the point.

I walk past the therapy wing. The windows are bright with daylight. Through the glass I can see a circle of chairs, empty now, waiting for Monday. The walls have been painted a warm yellow that looks almost orange in the afternoon light. There is no camera in the corner. I check. I always check.

I walk past the garden. The bench is damp from last night's rain. I touch the wood with my fingertips — rough, unfinished, the kind of bench that was built by hand rather than ordered from a catalog. Someone loved this bench into existence. Someone decided that this particular patch of ground deserved a place to sit.
I walk past the front door. I stop.

The photograph. The one of my mother. It used to hang in the entrance hall — Caroline Bly, young and luminous, standing in this exact doorway with the Scottish sky behind her. It was gone by the time I came back for the trial. I assumed someone had taken it down, packed it away, filed it under *Evidence* or *Loss*.

But it's here. Not in the hall — they've hung it beside the front door, in a simple wooden frame. Her face is turned slightly away from the camera, as though she's about to step outside. As though she's leaving.
Or arriving.
I look at her for a long time. She doesn't look back. Photographs never do. That's what makes them bearable.
The lake.

I don't walk to it. I walk past it — keeping the distance, keeping the shore between us, because some geography you respect. The water is calm today, the surface barely troubled by the wind. It looks ordinary. That's the most unsettling thing about it, and also the most merciful. The lake where Ms. Jessel walked in. The lake where Helen's evidence surfaced — the hard drive, the screenshots, the proof that turned everything inside out. It's just water now. Water doesn't hold grudges. Water doesn't remember.
I do.

But the remembering is different today. It sits lighter in my chest. Not gone — I don't think it will ever be gone — but settled, like sediment that's finally found the bottom. I can see through the water to the silt below. The lake is shallow near the shore. The mud is visible, dark and clean. Nothing is hiding down there.
I keep walking.
The tower room.

I haven't been up here since before the trial. The stairs are the same — narrow, spiraling, the stone worn smooth in the center from centuries of footsteps. My hand trails along the wall as I climb, and the stone is cool under my palm, rough with age, patient.
The door at the top is unlatched. It swings open easily.
The room is warm.

That's the first thing I notice — not the chill I remember, not the damp creeping in through the stone, but actual warmth, from a radiator under the window and sunlight pouring through glass that has been cleaned for the first time in decades. The memorial library. That's what they call it now. Shelves line the curved walls, filled with books about resilience and recovery, about trauma and hope, about the science of rebuilding a mind. There are novels, too — fiction, poetry, memoirs. The books are organized by no system I can detect, which feels right. A library in a tower room shouldn't follow rules. It should follow need.
In the center of the room, beneath the window, is a glass case.
I approach it slowly.

The case is small, no bigger than a bread box, with a brass latch and a small plaque that reads: *The Archive — Read With Caution.* Peter's books are inside — the three he wrote before the Game, the ones about behavioral architecture and systems design. They look thinner than I remember. Smaller. The spines are faded, the covers plain. They are the work of a man who thought he understood people because he'd learned to predict them. He was wrong. Prediction is not understanding. Control is not care.
But the books aren't what I came for.
Beneath them, in a protective sleeve, is the letter.

Peter's letter to Caroline. The one he never sent. The one I found in his desk after the arrest, folded into quarters with a crease so sharp it nearly cut the paper in two. I've read it before. I've read it a dozen times. I've carried it with me, and I've left it behind, and I've come back for it, and I've wished it didn't exist.

I unlace the brass latch. The glass lifts on quiet hinges. I slide the sleeve out and open it, and the paper is soft between my fingers — old and worn from handling, from my handling, all the times I've unfolded it and searched for something that was never there.
The letter is short. Peter was never at a loss for words except when they mattered most.
*Caroline,*
*I know this will never reach you. I know I won't send it. I know that knowing and doing are different things, and I have always chosen the wrong one.*

*I'm sorry for the house. I'm sorry for what it became. I wanted to build something that would hold us together, and instead I built something that watches. I see that now. I see it too late, as I see everything — after the damage is done and the evidence is irrefutable, because evidence was always the only language I trusted.*
*You were right about Bly. You were right about me. I wish I had been the kind of man who could hear you.*
*Peter*
I fold the letter along its creases. The paper is so thin now that I can feel the fibers separating under my thumbs. One more reading and it might come apart entirely.
I could keep it.

The thought comes quiet and reasonable, the way thoughts always do when they're trying to convince you of something you shouldn't do. It's my father's only words to my mother. It's the sole artifact of whatever passed between them that wasn't architecture, wasn't strategy, wasn't the Game. It's proof that somewhere inside Peter Bly — buried beneath the surveillance and the manipulation and the endless, pathological need to observe — there was a man who knew he was wrong and couldn't stop himself from being wrong anyway.
But keeping it would be holding on. And holding on is what Peter did.

He built the Game because he couldn't let go of control. He designed the screens because he couldn't bear not to watch. He turned the house into a mechanism of observation because the alternative — trusting people, releasing them, letting them leave — was unthinkable to him. He held on until his grip destroyed everything he was trying to keep.

I am my father's daughter. I know this. I've known it since I sat in that courtroom and watched the prosecution play the recordings and heard my own voice, younger and more certain than I'd ever been, narrating evidence that sent him to prison. I know it in the way I check corners for cameras, in the way I catalog exits, in the way I sometimes catch myself watching people instead of talking to them — studying them, filing away details, building profiles. The instinct is there. It will always be there.
But instinct is not destiny.
I place the letter back in its sleeve. I lay the sleeve in the case beneath the books. I close the glass. The brass latch clicks shut with a sound like a period at the end of a sentence.
The story belongs to the house now. And the house belongs to the people it once hurt.

I am not Peter. I don't need to own this. I don't need to carry it in my pocket like a talisman or a wound. The letter is here, in the place where it happened, in the room where the screens once glowed with other people's private lives. It's where it belongs. Not with me.
The window.
I turn to it before I leave, because there is one more thing, and I owe it to myself to face it.

The tower window looks out over the lake. The glass is clean — someone has made sure of that, someone has wiped away years of grime and neglect so that the view is clear, almost shockingly so. I can see the water, the far shore, the hills beyond, the sky pressing down on everything with the weight of a Scottish afternoon — gray and white and that strange, luminous near-blue that only happens at this latitude.
The lake surface is still. Mirror-still.
And for a moment — just a moment, the length of a held breath — I see my own reflection in it.

Not an impossible angle. Not a ghost. Not the trick of a camera or a surveillance feed or a screen showing me back to myself in real time. Just the ordinary physics of light and water and a woman standing in a tower window, looking down.
Maeve Bly.
Peter's daughter. Caroline's daughter. Helen's friend. Flora's sister. A woman who walked into a house that eats people and walked out again. Not whole. Not unscathed. But walking.
The reflection is accurate.

I study it the way I've studied everything in this house — carefully, systematically, looking for the flaw, the tell, the evidence that what I'm seeing isn't real. But there's nothing hidden in this image. No double exposure. No camera tricks. No pixels. Just a face — mine — with the scar above my left eyebrow from the night in the basement, and the lines around my eyes that weren't there three years ago, and the mouth that finally, after everything, knows how to rest without pressing itself into a neutral expression for an audience.

I'd been so afraid of what I'd see when I looked at myself honestly. That's why I could never stop watching. That's why the screens held me — not because Peter designed them to, but because I needed them. I needed the mediation. I needed the distance. I needed to see myself through a lens, through a narrative, through the story I was telling about myself in real time, because the alternative was looking directly and finding something I couldn't survive.
But it's just me.
Flawed. Scarred. Alive.

The water ripples — a bird, maybe, or the wind — and the reflection fragments into a thousand pieces, each one carrying a fraction of my face, and then the surface stills again and I'm gone. The lake shows me sky and hills and nothing human at all.
That's all right.
I don't need to see myself anymore. I was never the kind of mirror the world required me to be.
I turn from the window.

The tower room is quiet. The books wait on their shelves, patient as the stone walls, patient as the house itself. The glass case holds its small archive — my father's work, my father's words, my father's failure. Preserved. Catalogued. Closed.

I walk down the stairs. My hand doesn't touch the wall this time. I don't need the steadying. The steps are steep and old and they know my weight, and I place my feet carefully, the way you walk when you're leaving somewhere for the last time — not rushing, not lingering, just moving.

The front door of Bly Manor is open. The afternoon spills through it, gold and gray, that particular Scottish light that can't decide whether it's sunny or overcast and settles for being both at once. I step over the threshold.

The door closes behind me. Not with a slam — with a soft, deliberate click. The sound of something concluding. The sound of a house that has learned, finally, to let go of the people who pass through it.
And the screen goes dark.

Not to static. Not to black. To a calm, deliberate blank — the blank of a monitor turned off, of a story that has reached its final page, of a window that no longer has anyone on the other side watching. The screen I've been watching myself on. The one the internet watched me on. The one Peter designed and the twins maintained and I turned against them with, the one that showed me to myself in angles I never chose, the one that made me a narrator before I was a person.
It's off.
The story is over.

I walk down the drive. The gravel is loose beneath my shoes, crunching softly with each step. The oak trees arch overhead, and their leaves are full and green, and the air smells like rain that hasn't fallen yet — clean and sharp and alive. Cold, too. The kind of cold that wakes you up, that reminds you that you have a body, that you are standing in it, that the ground is real and the sky is real and you are here, unwatched, unrecorded, free.
My phone buzzes.
I stop. Pull it from my jacket pocket. The screen lights up — small, ordinary, a rectangle of pale light in my palm.
A text from Helen.
*Having coffee. Doing a puzzle. Wish you were here.*

I read it twice. The first time for the words. The second time for the person behind them — Helen, who survived, who wrote a memoir, who drinks coffee and does puzzles on a Saturday afternoon because that is what living looks like after you've been through something that should have killed you. Simple things. Small things. The texture of an ordinary day.
I smile.
I type: *Next week.*

I put the phone away. The screen goes dark again — the small one, in my hand. The other screen, the one that's been running in my head for three years, stays dark. No afterimage. No burn-in. Just blank.
I keep walking.

The drive curves toward the main road. The house recedes behind me — stone and glass and memory, holding its ghosts the way a garden holds roots: quietly, underground, in the dark where they belong. The people inside are safe. The center will open again on Monday. The books will be read. The letter will stay in its case. The garden will grow.
And Maeve Bly walks away into a world that doesn't have a camera on her.
She's free.
The screen stays dark.
